escapeIntro: Socrates has locked his study and left four challenges. Take the first letter of each answer and spell what every philosopher hopes to become.

escapeStations.title: The Unshaken School
escapeStations.prompt: A school of ancient philosophy that taught calm acceptance of fate and mastery over the passions; its name now means coolly enduring hardship.
escapeStations.hint: Marcus Aurelius was one.

escapeStations.title: The Cradle of Thought
escapeStations.prompt: Rearrange HASTEN to the Greek city where Socrates questioned everyone he met.
escapeStations.hint: The home of the Academy.
escapeStations.reveal: HASTEN is an anagram of ATHENS.

escapeStations.title: The Stinging Insect
escapeStations.prompt: Socrates called himself this insect, sent to sting a sluggish Athens awake with awkward questions.
escapeStations.hint: A biting fly that pesters cattle.

escapeStations.title: The Study of Conduct
escapeStations.prompt: Shift each letter back 3 to read the branch of philosophy about right and wrong: H W K L F V
escapeStations.hint: To shift a letter back, step earlier in the alphabet — e.g. D back 3 is A (D→C→B→A). Aristotle wrote one 'to Nicomachus'.
escapeStations.reveal: H W K L F V shifted back 3 spells ETHICS.

escapeFinal.rule: Take the first letter of each answer, top to bottom.
escapeFinal.prompt: Spell the word for a wise thinker that unlocks the study.
escapeFinal.hint: Also the name of a herb.

escapeIntro: The map-maker's study locks tight at nightfall. Five charts each yield a letter — but the lock won't take them in order. Gather them, then set them straight.

escapeStations.title: Narrow Waters
escapeStations.prompt: Rearrange TRAITS to a narrow channel of water between two seas.
escapeStations.hint: A narrow sea passage.
escapeStations.reveal: TRAITS is an anagram of STRAIT.

escapeStations.title: The Equator Lines
escapeStations.prompt: The angular distance of a place north or south of the equator.
escapeStations.hint: Lines that run east–west.

escapeStations.title: The Coral Ring
escapeStations.prompt: Shift each letter back 1 to read a ring-shaped coral island: B U P M M
escapeStations.hint: To shift a letter back, step earlier in the alphabet — e.g. D back 3 is A (D→C→B→A). Bikini is a famous one.
escapeStations.reveal: B U P M M shifted back 1 spells ATOLL.

escapeStations.title: The Hot Zone
escapeStations.prompt: I am a line of latitude that names a sweltering zone; the Tropic of Cancer is one of me.
escapeStations.hint: Cancer and Capricorn each have one.

escapeStations.title: The Compass Bearing
escapeStations.prompt: A horizontal bearing measured clockwise from due north, in degrees.
escapeStations.hint: From an Arabic word for "the directions" — a surveyor’s term.

escapeFinal.rule: Each answer gives its first letter — but jumbled. Unscramble the five letters.
escapeFinal.prompt: Unscramble them to name the book every map-maker binds together.
escapeFinal.hint: Also the Titan condemned to hold up the sky.

escapeIntro: You're sealed inside the logician's workshop. Six puzzles each conceal a letter, scattered out of order — and not all of them give up their secret plainly.

escapeStations.title: A Question of Character
escapeStations.prompt: Rearrange MOLAR to a word meaning ethical — concerning right conduct.
escapeStations.hint: The opposite of immoral.
escapeStations.reveal: MOLAR is an anagram of MORAL.

escapeStations.title: The Bath-time Cry
escapeStations.prompt: Archimedes is said to have leapt from his bath shouting this word — Greek for "I have found it!" — on grasping how to measure volume.
escapeStations.hint: A cry of sudden discovery.

escapeStations.title: The Numbered Direction
escapeStations.prompt: A code of numbers, each the position of a letter in the alphabet — decode the direction a compass needle seeks: 14 15 18 20 8
escapeStations.hint: How it works: each number is a letter’s position in the alphabet — 1=A, 2=B, 3=C, … 26=Z. Count to that position for each number, then read the letters in order. A cardinal direction.

escapeStations.title: Socrates’ Weapon
escapeStations.prompt: A fixed shift through the alphabet hides Socrates’ favourite tactic — feigning ignorance to expose another’s. Crack it: N W T S D
escapeStations.hint: How to crack it: every letter has moved the same number of steps along the alphabet. Try shifting each letter back by 1, then 2, then 3… until the letters read as a real word. A figure of speech; he pretended to know nothing.
escapeStations.reveal: N W T S D shifted back 5 spells IRONY.

escapeStations.title: The Spinning Sphere
escapeStations.prompt: A spherical model of the Earth that sits on a desk and turns on its axis.
escapeStations.hint: It spins on a stand.

escapeStations.title: Reason’s Starting Point
escapeStations.prompt: A statement accepted as self-evidently true, taken without proof as the foundation of a logical system.
escapeStations.hint: Euclid began his geometry with these.

escapeFinal.rule: Collect the six first letters — they arrive scrambled. Rearrange them.
escapeFinal.prompt: Unscramble them to name a baffling mystery.
escapeFinal.hint: Also the famous WWII cipher machine the Allies raced to crack.

escapeIntro: Torchlight flickers across the Oracle of Delphi's antechamber — the same Oracle that once named Socrates the wisest of all. Six relics each surrender a letter, but the final lock does not read them as they are.

escapeStations.title: The Art of Argument
escapeStations.prompt: The branch of philosophy concerned with valid reasoning — distinguishing sound arguments from fallacies.
escapeStations.hint: Aristotle is its father.

escapeStations.title: The Perfect Form
escapeStations.prompt: A code of numbers, each the position of a letter in the alphabet — decode what Plato called the perfect, eternal Forms behind all things: 9 4 5 1
escapeStations.hint: How it works: each number is a letter’s position in the alphabet — 1=A, 2=B, 3=C, … 26=Z. Count to that position for each number, then read the letters in order. A thought; Plato’s Forms.

escapeStations.title: The Paradox-Maker
escapeStations.prompt: The philosopher of Elea famous for paradoxes of motion — Achilles can never catch the tortoise.
escapeStations.hint: Four letters; a Greek of Elea.

escapeStations.title: The Loyal Student
escapeStations.prompt: A soldier and historian, a devoted follower of Socrates, who recorded his teacher’s words in the Memorabilia.
escapeStations.hint: He also led ten thousand Greek mercenaries home — the Anabasis.

escapeStations.title: Mere Belief
escapeStations.prompt: In Plato, mere belief or appearance (doxa), as opposed to true and certain knowledge.
escapeStations.hint: Everyone has one; few have knowledge.

escapeStations.title: Aristotle’s Excellence
escapeStations.prompt: A fixed shift through the alphabet hides the excellence of character Aristotle placed at the golden mean. Crack it: E R A C D N
escapeStations.hint: How to crack it: every letter has moved the same number of steps along the alphabet. Try shifting each letter back by 1, then 2, then 3… until the letters read as a real word. Courage and temperance are examples; find the shift.
escapeStations.reveal: E R A C D N shifted back 9 spells VIRTUE.

escapeFinal.rule: Hold the alphabet up to a mirror — the first letter wears the mask of the last (A↔Z, B↔Y) — and reflect the six letters.
escapeFinal.prompt: Reflect them to name the Delphic seer who called Socrates the wisest of men.
escapeFinal.hint: A prophet who answers questions; mirror A↔Z.

escapeIntro: A small iron safe sits on the desk, its four dials waiting. Solve each clue for a single digit, then read them top to bottom.

escapeStations.title: The Chiselled Numeral
escapeStations.prompt: What digit is the Roman numeral III, carved above the door?
escapeStations.hint: How Roman numerals work: I=1, V=5, X=10, L=50, C=100, D=500, M=1000. A smaller numeral before a larger one subtracts (IV = 4); otherwise you add (VI = 6). Count the strokes.

escapeStations.title: The Sisters of Song
escapeStations.prompt: How many Muses did the ancient Greeks say inspired the arts and sciences?
escapeStations.hint: A cat is said to have this many lives.

escapeStations.title: The Ledger Sum
escapeStations.prompt: What is 5 + 4 - 7?
escapeStations.hint: Work left to right.

escapeStations.title: The Square Frame
escapeStations.prompt: How many sides does a square have?
escapeStations.hint: Count the corners.

escapeFinal.rule: Read the four digits from top to bottom.
escapeFinal.prompt: Enter the four-digit combination.
escapeFinal.hint: Just the digits, in order.

escapeIntro: Five tumblers each hide a digit — but this vault only opens when the digits are set in order. Find all five, then line them up from smallest to largest.

escapeStations.title: The Binary Plate
escapeStations.prompt: Convert the binary number 1000 to a decimal digit, etched on a brass plate.
escapeStations.hint: How binary works: it is base-2. Reading right to left, the columns are worth 1, 2, 4, 8, 16… Add up the column values wherever there is a 1 — for example 1101 = 8 + 4 + 0 + 1 = 13. Powers of two: 8, 4, 2, 1.

escapeStations.title: The Graces
escapeStations.prompt: How many Graces (Charites) attended Aphrodite in Greek myth?
escapeStations.hint: Aglaia, Euphrosyne and Thalia — count the names.

escapeStations.title: The Cube
escapeStations.prompt: How many faces does a cube have?
escapeStations.hint: Like a die.

escapeStations.title: The Idle Number
escapeStations.prompt: Multiplying any number by this leaves it completely unchanged.
escapeStations.hint: The multiplicative identity.

escapeStations.title: The Classical Elements
escapeStations.prompt: Empedocles taught that all things are made from this many elements: earth, water, air and fire.
escapeStations.hint: Earth, water, air, fire.

escapeFinal.rule: The five digits come out jumbled — arrange them from smallest to largest.
escapeFinal.prompt: Enter the five digits in ascending order.
escapeFinal.hint: Smallest digit first.

escapeIntro: Six dials, and a note: 'I have turned every digit forward by two.' Solve each clue, then wind the whole code back to where it began.

escapeStations.title: The Binary Cog
escapeStations.prompt: Convert the binary number 101 to a decimal digit, stamped inside the case.
escapeStations.hint: How binary works: it is base-2. Reading right to left, the columns are worth 1, 2, 4, 8, 16… Add up the column values wherever there is a 1 — for example 1101 = 8 + 4 + 0 + 1 = 13. Powers of two: 4 + 1.

escapeStations.title: The Fates
escapeStations.prompt: How many Fates (Moirai) spun, measured and cut the thread of life in Greek myth?
escapeStations.hint: Clotho, Lachesis, Atropos — count them.

escapeStations.title: The Honeycomb
escapeStations.prompt: How many sides does a hexagon have?
escapeStations.hint: Like a honeycomb cell — count its walls.

escapeStations.title: The Trivium
escapeStations.prompt: The Trivium of the classical liberal arts — grammar, logic and rhetoric — comprises how many subjects?
escapeStations.hint: Count the subjects listed.

escapeStations.title: The Week
escapeStations.prompt: How many days are there in a week?
escapeStations.hint: Monday through Sunday.

escapeStations.title: The Daylight Star
escapeStations.prompt: How many stars sit at the centre of our solar system?
escapeStations.hint: Look up by day.

escapeFinal.rule: Every digit was turned forward by 2 (past 9 rolls back to 0). Wind each digit back by 2.
escapeFinal.prompt: Enter the six-digit code once you've wound it back.
escapeFinal.hint: Wound back correctly, a famous circle constant appears.

escapeIntro: The final lock bears no instructions — only a note: 'Every digit has stepped forward by the same secret amount.' Solve the six clues, discover the step, and wind them all back.

escapeStations.title: Aristotle's Causes
escapeStations.prompt: Aristotle held that a full explanation of anything needs how many kinds of cause — material, formal, efficient and final?
escapeStations.hint: Material, formal, efficient, final.

escapeStations.title: The Binary Seal
escapeStations.prompt: Convert the binary number 1001 to a decimal digit, pressed into the wax.
escapeStations.hint: How binary works: it is base-2. Reading right to left, the columns are worth 1, 2, 4, 8, 16… Add up the column values wherever there is a 1 — for example 1101 = 8 + 4 + 0 + 1 = 13. Powers of two: 8 + 1.

escapeStations.title: The Square
escapeStations.prompt: What is 2 × 2?
escapeStations.hint: A number times itself.

escapeStations.title: The Single Stroke
escapeStations.prompt: What digit is the Roman numeral I, the smallest numeral?
escapeStations.hint: How Roman numerals work: I=1, V=5, X=10, L=50, C=100, D=500, M=1000. A smaller numeral before a larger one subtracts (IV = 4); otherwise you add (VI = 6). One line.

escapeStations.title: Three Angles
escapeStations.prompt: How many sides does a triangle have?
escapeStations.hint: Count the corners.

escapeStations.title: The Perfect Number
escapeStations.prompt: The Pythagoreans prized this smallest "perfect number", whose divisors 1, 2 and 3 add up to itself.

escapeFinal.rule: Every digit has stepped forward by the same secret amount, past 9 rolling back to 0. Find the step and wind them all back.
escapeFinal.prompt: Enter the six-digit code once you've found the step and reversed it.
escapeFinal.hint: Wound back correctly, the code begins with 1 — and a famously golden number hides within.

escapeIntro: The office door locks behind you. Four scraps of a case file each hide a word — take the first letter of each to name what you must crack.

escapeStations.title: The Backwards Memo
escapeStations.prompt: Written backwards — reverse it to find what every detective hunts for: E U L C
escapeStations.hint: How it works: read the letters from right to left. Four letters.

escapeStations.title: The Numbered Alibi
escapeStations.prompt: A code of numbers, each the position of a letter in the alphabet — decode a suspect's claim to have been elsewhere: 1 12 9 2 9
escapeStations.hint: How it works: each number is a letter’s position in the alphabet — 1=A, 2=B, 3=C, … 26=Z. Count to that position for each number, then read the letters in order. A is 1.

escapeStations.title: The Coded Culprit
escapeStations.prompt: Shift each letter back 2 to read the person the police are watching: U W U R G E V
escapeStations.hint: To shift a letter back, step earlier in the alphabet — e.g. D back 3 is A (D→C→B→A). Under suspicion.
escapeStations.reveal: U W U R G E V shifted back 2 spells SUSPECT.

escapeStations.title: The Trace Left Behind
escapeStations.prompt: Fingerprints, fibres and footprints gathered at a crime scene are all this.
escapeStations.hint: Proof of what happened.

escapeFinal.rule: Take the first letter of each answer, top to bottom.
escapeFinal.prompt: Spell what a detective works to crack.
escapeFinal.hint: A mystery to solve; four letters.

escapeIntro: Alarms blare on the bridge. Five instruments each read out a digit — but the airlock only opens when they're set in order, smallest to largest.

escapeStations.title: The Hull Plate
escapeStations.prompt: What is the square root of 81?
escapeStations.hint: How it works: find the number that, multiplied by itself, gives this value. A plate is stamped with a square number.

escapeStations.title: The Warning Lights
escapeStations.prompt: What single digit continues the pattern (they fall by two): 9, 7, 5, ?
escapeStations.hint: Count down.

escapeStations.title: The Reactor Code
escapeStations.prompt: Add the digits of 88, then keep adding until a single digit remains — its digital root.
escapeStations.hint: How it works: sum the digits; if that still has more than one digit, sum again, and repeat until one digit is left. Reduce it to one digit.

escapeStations.title: The Docking Clock
escapeStations.prompt: A clock reads 10 o'clock. What hour will it show 4 hours later, on a 12-hour clock?
escapeStations.hint: A twelve-hour dial.

escapeStations.title: The Ship Name
escapeStations.prompt: How many letters are in the word ROCKET, painted on the hull?
escapeStations.hint: Count the letters.

escapeFinal.rule: The five digits come through jumbled — set them smallest to largest.
escapeFinal.prompt: Enter the airlock code with the digits in ascending order.
escapeFinal.hint: Smallest digit first.

escapeIntro: The library doors slam shut. Five mouldering volumes each surrender a letter, scrambled and encoded — decode them all, then rearrange the letters.

escapeStations.title: The Vowelless Sign
escapeStations.prompt: The vowels have been stripped out — put them back to find a foreboding sign of things to come: M N
escapeStations.hint: How it works: only the consonants remain. Slot the vowels A, E, I, O, U back in until it reads as a word. A bad sign.
escapeStations.reveal: Restoring the vowels of MN spells OMEN.

escapeStations.title: The Stone Chamber
escapeStations.prompt: A stone vault where the dead are laid to rest.
escapeStations.hint: A grave or crypt.

escapeStations.title: The Zig-Zag Epitaph
escapeStations.prompt: A two-row rail-fence cipher — unzip it to find a grave-robbing spirit: G O L H U
escapeStations.hint: How a rail-fence works: the letters were written in a two-row zig-zag (1st on top, 2nd on the bottom, 3rd on top…), then read off — all of the top row, then all of the bottom row. Split the code in half and interleave the two halves back together. It haunts graveyards.
escapeStations.reveal: Interleaving the two rows of GOLHU spells GHOUL.

escapeStations.title: The Mirror Writing
escapeStations.prompt: Written backwards — reverse it to find another word for a ghost or spectre: E D A H S
escapeStations.hint: How it works: read the letters from right to left. A lingering spirit.

escapeStations.title: The Keyed Curse
escapeStations.prompt: A Vigenère cipher with the keyword MOON — decode what a restless ghost does to a house: T O I A F
escapeStations.hint: How Vigenère works: write the keyword under the code, repeating it. Each key letter is a shift amount (A=0, B=1, C=2 …); shift each code letter back by its key letter to reveal the plaintext. Ghosts do this to houses.
escapeStations.reveal: Decoding TOIAF with key MOON spells HAUNT.

escapeFinal.rule: The five first letters come out jumbled — rearrange them.
escapeFinal.prompt: Unscramble them to name what walks the library at night.
escapeFinal.hint: A spooky spectre; five letters.

escapeIntro: You're inside the vault with minutes to spare. Six dials, and a scrawled warning: 'Every digit has crept forward by the same secret amount.' Solve them, find the step, and wind them back.

escapeStations.title: The Third Tumbler
escapeStations.prompt: What is the 3rd prime number?
escapeStations.hint: How it works: primes are whole numbers greater than 1 divisible only by 1 and themselves — 2, 3, 5, 7, 11…

escapeStations.title: The Leftover Dial
escapeStations.prompt: What is the remainder when 23 is divided by 6?
escapeStations.hint: How it works: divide and keep only what is left over (the modulo).

escapeStations.title: The Arrangement Lock
escapeStations.prompt: In how many different orders can 3 distinct items be lined up (that is, 3 factorial)?
escapeStations.hint: How it works: multiply every whole number from 1 up to n — for example 3 factorial = 1 × 2 × 3 = 6.

escapeStations.title: The Root Dial
escapeStations.prompt: What is the square root of 49?
escapeStations.hint: How it works: find the number that, multiplied by itself, gives this value.

escapeStations.title: The Reduced Code
escapeStations.prompt: Add the digits of 999, then keep adding until a single digit remains — its digital root.
escapeStations.hint: How it works: sum the digits; if that still has more than one digit, sum again, and repeat until one digit is left.

escapeStations.title: The Countdown Timer
escapeStations.prompt: A clock reads 11 o'clock. What hour will it show 3 hours later, on a 12-hour clock?

escapeFinal.rule: Every digit has crept forward by the same secret amount, past 9 rolling back to 0. Find the step and wind them all back.
escapeFinal.prompt: Enter the six-digit vault code once you've reversed the step.
escapeFinal.hint: Wound back correctly, every digit from the third onward is the sum of the two before it.

escapeIntro: A storm rattles the lighthouse door and the latch clicks shut. Four objects of the keeper's trade each hide a word — take the first letter of each.

escapeStations.title: The Curved Glass
escapeStations.prompt: The great curved glass at the top of the tower that gathers the light into a single beam.
escapeStations.hint: A camera has one too.

escapeStations.title: The Heavy Hook
escapeStations.prompt: Dropped from a ship on a chain to hold her fast against the tide.
escapeStations.hint: Hoisted when a ship sets sail.

escapeStations.title: The Backwards Log
escapeStations.prompt: Written backwards — reverse it to find the tall pole that carries a ship’s sails: T S A M
escapeStations.hint: How it works: read the letters from right to left. Look up from the deck.

escapeStations.title: The Harbour Word
escapeStations.prompt: Shift each letter back 2 to read a safe harbour — and the left side of a ship: R Q T V
escapeStations.hint: To shift a letter back, step earlier in the alphabet — e.g. D back 3 is A (D→C→B→A). Opposite of starboard.
escapeStations.reveal: R Q T V shifted back 2 spells PORT.

escapeFinal.rule: Take the first letter of each answer, top to bottom.
escapeFinal.prompt: Spell what the keeper lights at dusk.
escapeFinal.hint: It burns at the top of the tower.

escapeIntro: The baker stepped out and the till locked itself. Four notes pinned to the counter each yield a digit — read them top to bottom.

escapeStations.title: The Recipe Card
escapeStations.prompt: How many letters are in the word BISCUIT, written on the recipe card?
escapeStations.hint: Count every letter.

escapeStations.title: The Batch Count
escapeStations.prompt: What is 18 / 9?
escapeStations.hint: Loaves per tray.

escapeStations.title: The Egg Basket
escapeStations.prompt: How many eggs are in half a dozen?
escapeStations.hint: A dozen is twelve.

escapeStations.title: The Cooling Racks
escapeStations.prompt: What single digit continues the pattern (each rack holds four fewer): 16, 12, 8, ?
escapeStations.hint: Keep subtracting the same amount.

escapeFinal.rule: Read the four digits from top to bottom.
escapeFinal.prompt: Enter the four-digit code to open the till.
escapeFinal.hint: Just the digits, in order.

escapeIntro: An old toy chest with a four-dial lock. Four toys each carry a digit — read them top to bottom.

escapeStations.title: The Painted Block
escapeStations.prompt: What digit is the Roman numeral V, painted on a wooden block?
escapeStations.hint: How Roman numerals work: I=1, V=5, X=10, L=50, C=100, D=500, M=1000. A smaller numeral before a larger one subtracts (IV = 4); otherwise you add (VI = 6). One letter, one numeral.

escapeStations.title: The Toy Robot
escapeStations.prompt: Convert the binary number 110 to a decimal digit, stamped under the toy robot.
escapeStations.hint: How binary works: it is base-2. Reading right to left, the columns are worth 1, 2, 4, 8, 16… Add up the column values wherever there is a 1 — for example 1101 = 8 + 4 + 0 + 1 = 13. Two columns light up.

escapeStations.title: The Tricycle
escapeStations.prompt: How many wheels does a tricycle have?
escapeStations.hint: The name says it — "tri".

escapeStations.title: The Kite String
escapeStations.prompt: Add the digits of 10, then keep adding until a single digit remains — its digital root.
escapeStations.hint: How it works: sum the digits; if that still has more than one digit, sum again, and repeat until one digit is left. The tag on the kite string reads 10.

escapeFinal.rule: Read the four digits from top to bottom.
escapeFinal.prompt: Enter the four-digit code to open the chest.
escapeFinal.hint: Just the digits, in order.

escapeIntro: The marketplace empties and the gate bars itself behind you. Five stalls each yield a letter — but the gate reads them from the last stall back to the first.

escapeStations.title: The Sworn Word
escapeStations.prompt: A solemn promise, sworn before the gods, that a Greek would sooner die than break.
escapeStations.hint: Witnesses swear one in court.

escapeStations.title: The Numbered Ideal
escapeStations.prompt: A code of numbers, each the position of a letter in the alphabet — decode what every honest argument aims at: 20 18 21 20 8
escapeStations.hint: How it works: each number is a letter’s position in the alphabet — 1=A, 2=B, 3=C, … 26=Z. Count to that position for each number, then read the letters in order. What the honest tongue tells.

escapeStations.title: The Open Square
escapeStations.prompt: The open marketplace where Socrates buttonholed passers-by with questions.
escapeStations.hint: The civic heart of a Greek city.

escapeStations.title: The Rival School
escapeStations.prompt: The school Aristotle founded in Athens, where he lectured while strolling its walks.
escapeStations.hint: Its students were called the Peripatetics — the walkers.

escapeStations.title: The Hidden Soul
escapeStations.prompt: Shift each letter back 4 to read the Greek word for the soul: T W C G L I
escapeStations.hint: To shift a letter back, step earlier in the alphabet — e.g. D back 3 is A (D→C→B→A). It gives psychology its name.
escapeStations.reveal: T W C G L I shifted back 4 spells PSYCHE.

escapeFinal.rule: Take the first letter of each answer — then read them from the last station up to the first.
escapeFinal.prompt: Name the philosopher whose dialogues made Socrates immortal.
escapeFinal.hint: Student of Socrates, teacher of Aristotle.

escapeIntro: The dome closes for the night with you inside. Five instruments each yield a letter — but they come out jumbled. Collect them, then rearrange.

escapeStations.title: The Silver Disc
escapeStations.prompt: Earth's only natural satellite, keeper of the tides.
escapeStations.hint: Full once a month.

escapeStations.title: The Numbered Path
escapeStations.prompt: A code of numbers, each the position of a letter in the alphabet — decode the path a planet traces around its star: 15 18 2 9 20
escapeStations.hint: How it works: each number is a letter’s position in the alphabet — 1=A, 2=B, 3=C, … 26=Z. Count to that position for each number, then read the letters in order. An ellipse, said Kepler.

escapeStations.title: The Pocked Surface
escapeStations.prompt: Rearrange TRACER to a bowl-shaped scar left by an impact.
escapeStations.hint: The Moon is covered in them.
escapeStations.reveal: TRACER is an anagram of CRATER.

escapeStations.title: The Long Eye
escapeStations.prompt: An instrument of lenses and mirrors for viewing the distant sky.
escapeStations.hint: Galileo turned one on Jupiter.

escapeStations.title: The Shadowed Sun
escapeStations.prompt: Shift each letter back 3 to read when one heavenly body hides another: H F O L S V H
escapeStations.hint: To shift a letter back, step earlier in the alphabet — e.g. D back 3 is A (D→C→B→A). Never look at one directly.
escapeStations.reveal: H F O L S V H shifted back 3 spells ECLIPSE.

escapeFinal.rule: The five first letters come out jumbled — rearrange them.
escapeFinal.prompt: Unscramble them to name the dirty snowball with the glowing tail.
escapeFinal.hint: Halley's is the most famous.

escapeIntro: The last train is locked until departure. Four tickets each carry a digit — but the board reads them from the last ticket back to the first.

escapeStations.title: The Empty Seat
escapeStations.prompt: How many even prime numbers are greater than two?
escapeStations.hint: Every even number above two can be split in half.
escapeStations.reveal: None — every even number above 2 is divisible by 2, so 0.

escapeStations.title: The Platform Sign
escapeStations.prompt: What is the 3rd prime number?
escapeStations.hint: How it works: primes are whole numbers greater than 1 divisible only by 1 and themselves — 2, 3, 5, 7, 11… The platform bears the third of a famous family of numbers.

escapeStations.title: The Ticket Stub
escapeStations.prompt: What is the remainder when 14 is divided by 4?
escapeStations.hint: How it works: divide and keep only what is left over (the modulo).

escapeStations.title: The Punched Card
escapeStations.prompt: Convert the binary number 1 to a decimal digit, punched into the card.
escapeStations.hint: How binary works: it is base-2. Reading right to left, the columns are worth 1, 2, 4, 8, 16… Add up the column values wherever there is a 1 — for example 1101 = 8 + 4 + 0 + 1 = 13.

escapeFinal.rule: Read the four digits from the last station back to the first.
escapeFinal.prompt: Enter the departure time shown on the board.
escapeFinal.hint: Ten to one, just after midday.

escapeIntro: The trapdoor thuds shut above the alchemist's attic. Six curiosities each conceal a letter — encoded, disguised, and out of order.

escapeStations.title: The Faded Bestiary
escapeStations.prompt: The vowels have been stripped out — put them back to find the fire-breathing guardian of hoarded gold: D R G N
escapeStations.hint: How it works: only the consonants remain. Slot the vowels A, E, I, O, U back in until it reads as a word. It sleeps on treasure in the old tales.
escapeStations.reveal: Restoring the vowels of DRGN spells DRAGON.

escapeStations.title: The Zig-Zag Gem
escapeStations.prompt: A two-row rail-fence cipher — unzip it to find a milky gemstone that flashes many colours: O A P L
escapeStations.hint: How a rail-fence works: the letters were written in a two-row zig-zag (1st on top, 2nd on the bottom, 3rd on top…), then read off — all of the top row, then all of the bottom row. Split the code in half and interleave the two halves back together. An October birthstone.
escapeStations.reveal: Interleaving the two rows of OAPL spells OPAL.

escapeStations.title: The Quenching Element
escapeStations.prompt: One of the four classical elements — it quenches the second of them.
escapeStations.hint: Rivers and rain are made of it.

escapeStations.title: The Numbered Metal
escapeStations.prompt: A code of numbers, each the position of a letter in the alphabet — decode the stubborn metal the alchemists despaired of turning to gold: 9 18 15 14
escapeStations.hint: How it works: each number is a letter’s position in the alphabet — 1=A, 2=B, 3=C, … 26=Z. Count to that position for each number, then read the letters in order. Blacksmiths forge it.

escapeStations.title: The Keyed Element
escapeStations.prompt: A Vigenère cipher with the keyword FIRE — decode the yellow, evil-smelling element of the old laboratories: X C C J Z Z
escapeStations.hint: How Vigenère works: write the keyword under the code, repeating it. Each key letter is a shift amount (A=0, B=1, C=2 …); shift each code letter back by its key letter to reveal the plaintext. Brimstone, by its older name.
escapeStations.reveal: Decoding XCCJZZ with key FIRE spells SULFUR.

escapeStations.title: The Mirrored Metal
escapeStations.prompt: Written backwards — reverse it to find the silver metal that runs liquid at room temperature: Y R U C R E M
escapeStations.hint: How it works: read the letters from right to left. Quicksilver — and a planet.

escapeFinal.rule: The six first letters arrive scrambled — rearrange them.
escapeFinal.prompt: Unscramble them to name what the true alchemist sought to refine within.
escapeFinal.hint: Not gold — the owl of Athena symbolises it.

escapeIntro: The cellar door swings shut among the ticking. Six mechanisms each yield a digit — and a note: 'I wound every dial forward three.' Wind them back.

escapeStations.title: The Mantel Clock
escapeStations.prompt: A clock reads 2 o'clock. What hour will it show 3 hours later, on a 12-hour clock?
escapeStations.hint: Count around the dial.

escapeStations.title: The Odd Gears
escapeStations.prompt: What single digit continues the pattern (the gear teeth grow by two): 1, 3, 5, ?
escapeStations.hint: Keep adding the same amount.

escapeStations.title: The Serial Plate
escapeStations.prompt: Add the digits of 81, then keep adding until a single digit remains — its digital root.
escapeStations.hint: How it works: sum the digits; if that still has more than one digit, sum again, and repeat until one digit is left. The serial plate reads 81.

escapeStations.title: The Escapement
escapeStations.prompt: What is the remainder when 25 is divided by 8?
escapeStations.hint: How it works: divide and keep only what is left over (the modulo).

escapeStations.title: The Square Weight
escapeStations.prompt: What is the square root of 16?
escapeStations.hint: How it works: find the number that, multiplied by itself, gives this value. The weight is stamped 16.

escapeStations.title: The Second Spring
escapeStations.prompt: What is the 2nd prime number?
escapeStations.hint: How it works: primes are whole numbers greater than 1 divisible only by 1 and themselves — 2, 3, 5, 7, 11… The spring bears the second of a famous family of numbers.

escapeFinal.rule: Every dial was wound forward 3 (past 9 rolls back to 0). Wind each digit back by 3.
escapeFinal.prompt: Enter the six-digit code once every dial is wound back.
escapeFinal.hint: Wound back correctly, the clockmaker counts by twos.

escapeIntro: The hold hatch slams above you. Six pieces of plunder each carry a digit — and pirates being pirates, the lock wants them greediest-first.

escapeStations.title: The Odd One Out
escapeStations.prompt: What is the only even prime number?
escapeStations.hint: Every other even number has it as a divisor.

escapeStations.title: The Grog Barrel
escapeStations.prompt: How many letters are in the word RUM, branded on the barrel?
escapeStations.hint: Count the letters of the drink.

escapeStations.title: The Powder Keg
escapeStations.prompt: Convert the binary number 1000 to a decimal digit, burned into the powder keg.
escapeStations.hint: How binary works: it is base-2. Reading right to left, the columns are worth 1, 2, 4, 8, 16… Add up the column values wherever there is a 1 — for example 1101 = 8 + 4 + 0 + 1 = 13.

escapeStations.title: The Map Corner
escapeStations.prompt: Add the digits of 19, then keep adding until a single digit remains — its digital root.
escapeStations.hint: How it works: sum the digits; if that still has more than one digit, sum again, and repeat until one digit is left. A 19 is scratched into the map corner.

escapeStations.title: The Carved Mast
escapeStations.prompt: What digit is the Roman numeral V, carved deep in the mast?
escapeStations.hint: How Roman numerals work: I=1, V=5, X=10, L=50, C=100, D=500, M=1000. A smaller numeral before a larger one subtracts (IV = 4); otherwise you add (VI = 6). One letter of the old empire.

escapeStations.title: The Logbook Leagues
escapeStations.prompt: What single digit continues the pattern (the leagues fall by the same amount each day): 28, 21, 14, ?
escapeStations.hint: Keep subtracting.

escapeFinal.rule: Set the six digits from largest to smallest — greediest first.
escapeFinal.prompt: Enter the six-digit code to open the captain’s chest.
escapeFinal.hint: Biggest digit first, smallest last.

escapeIntro: Chains fall away and you turn from the cave wall at last — but the way out is barred. Six carvings each yield a letter, and the lock does not read them as they are.

escapeStations.title: The Utter Emptiness
escapeStations.prompt: What Democritus said atoms move through — sheer nothingness.
escapeStations.hint: Nature abhors a vacuum; this is one.

escapeStations.title: The Stripped Vision
escapeStations.prompt: The vowels have been stripped out — put them back to find a sudden flash of deep understanding: N S G H T
escapeStations.hint: How it works: only the consonants remain. Slot the vowels A, E, I, O, U back in until it reads as a word. Seeing into the heart of a problem.
escapeStations.reveal: Restoring the vowels of NSGHT spells INSIGHT.

escapeStations.title: The Numbered Nature
escapeStations.prompt: A code of numbers, each the position of a letter in the alphabet — decode what a thing most truly is, beneath all appearances: 5 19 19 5 14 3 5
escapeStations.hint: How it works: each number is a letter’s position in the alphabet — 1=A, 2=B, 3=C, … 26=Z. Count to that position for each number, then read the letters in order. Perfume-makers borrow the word.

escapeStations.title: The First Feeling
escapeStations.prompt: Aristotle said philosophy begins in this — the feeling that sets us questioning.
escapeStations.hint: Alice found a land of it.

escapeStations.title: The Zig-Zag Name
escapeStations.prompt: A two-row rail-fence cipher — unzip it to find the barefoot questioner of Athens: S C A E O R T S
escapeStations.hint: How a rail-fence works: the letters were written in a two-row zig-zag (1st on top, 2nd on the bottom, 3rd on top…), then read off — all of the top row, then all of the bottom row. Split the code in half and interleave the two halves back together. He drank the hemlock.
escapeStations.reveal: Interleaving the two rows of SCAEORTS spells SOCRATES.

escapeStations.title: The Keyed Truth
escapeStations.prompt: A Vigenère cipher with the keyword CAVE — decode what the prisoners in the cave never see directly: T E V P K T T
escapeStations.hint: How Vigenère works: write the keyword under the code, repeating it. Each key letter is a shift amount (A=0, B=1, C=2 …); shift each code letter back by its key letter to reveal the plaintext. The shadows are only its copy.
escapeStations.reveal: Decoding TEVPKTT with key CAVE spells REALITY.

escapeFinal.rule: The six letters stand one fixed shift away from the true word. Find the shift and step every letter back.
escapeFinal.prompt: Reveal the faculty Plato said could climb out of the cave.
escapeFinal.hint: Try small shifts first — stop when the letters read as a word for clear thinking.

escapeIntro: The gallery lights die and the doors seal. Five exhibits each surrender a letter — but the lock reads the alphabet in a mirror.

escapeStations.title: The Carved Tusk
escapeStations.prompt: The smooth white material of carved tusks in the antiquities case.
escapeStations.hint: Piano keys were once cut from it.

escapeStations.title: The Sealed Chamber
escapeStations.prompt: A sealed, guarded chamber where a museum keeps its greatest treasures.
escapeStations.hint: Banks have them too.

escapeStations.title: The Mirrored Frame
escapeStations.prompt: Written backwards — reverse it to find the shape of the gallery’s grandest frame: L A V O
escapeStations.hint: How it works: read the letters from right to left. An egg has this shape.

escapeStations.title: The Famous Stone
escapeStations.prompt: Shift each letter back 5 to read the stone that unlocked the hieroglyphs: W T X J Y Y F
escapeStations.hint: To shift a letter back, step earlier in the alphabet — e.g. D back 3 is A (D→C→B→A). Found near a delta town in 1799.
escapeStations.reveal: W T X J Y Y F shifted back 5 spells ROSETTA.

escapeStations.title: The Security Scan
escapeStations.prompt: A code of numbers, each the position of a letter in the alphabet — decode the scan every crate passes through at the door: 24 18 1 25
escapeStations.hint: How it works: each number is a letter’s position in the alphabet — 1=A, 2=B, 3=C, … 26=Z. Count to that position for each number, then read the letters in order. It sees through the packaging.

escapeFinal.rule: Each letter has traded places with its opposite end of the alphabet — the first with the last, the second with the second-from-last.
escapeFinal.prompt: Reflect the letters to name what every museum guards: an object left over from the deep past.
escapeFinal.hint: Swap A with Z, B with Y, C with X — and so on.

escapeIntro: The chess club's trophy vault clicks shut. Six positions each yield a digit — and a note in the grandmaster's hand: 'Every dial has advanced by the same secret step.'

escapeStations.title: The Fourth Rank
escapeStations.prompt: What is the 4th prime number?
escapeStations.hint: How it works: primes are whole numbers greater than 1 divisible only by 1 and themselves — 2, 3, 5, 7, 11… The lock wants the fourth of a famous family of numbers.

escapeStations.title: The Front Line
escapeStations.prompt: How many pawns does each side command at the start of a chess game?
escapeStations.hint: A full second rank of them.

escapeStations.title: The Faithful Bishop
escapeStations.prompt: How many squares can a bishop ever reach that are NOT its own starting colour?
escapeStations.hint: A bishop moves only on diagonals — it can never change square colour.
escapeStations.reveal: None — a bishop never leaves its colour, so 0.

escapeStations.title: The Smallest Piece
escapeStations.prompt: How many letters are in the word PAWN, the humblest piece on the board?
escapeStations.hint: Count the letters.

escapeStations.title: The Odd Files
escapeStations.prompt: What single digit continues the pattern (the files climb by two): 1, 3, 5, ?
escapeStations.hint: Keep adding the same amount.

escapeStations.title: The Paired Clergy
escapeStations.prompt: How many bishops does each side start with?
escapeStations.hint: One for each square colour.

escapeFinal.rule: Every dial has advanced by the same secret step (past 9 wrapping to 0). Find the step and wind them all back.
escapeFinal.prompt: Enter the six-digit code once you have found and reversed the step.
escapeFinal.hint: Wound back correctly, each of the first four digits is double the one before it.
